text.title: prometheus-monitoring Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for prometheus-monitoring. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Prefer Histogram over Summary for latency metrics in distributed systems.
rule.rationale: Histograms are aggregatable across replicas with sum(rate(...)) by (le). Summary quantiles are calculated per-instance and cannot be meaningfully combined across multiple pods.

rule.statement: Follow the pattern: namespace_name_unit_suffix. Use snake_case. Suffix counters with _total; histograms with _seconds or _bytes.
rule.rationale: Consistent naming allows tooling (Grafana, alerting) to auto-discover metrics and enables teams to understand a metric's purpose without documentation.

rule.statement: Never use unbounded values as labels: no user IDs, request IDs, email addresses, or UUIDs. Each unique label combination creates a new time series.
rule.rationale: High cardinality causes Prometheus OOM crashes. 1M users × 10 endpoints = 10M time series from a single metric. Labels must have bounded value sets: HTTP methods, status code classes, service names.

rule.statement: Keep label count per metric at or below 10. Add only labels needed for meaningful aggregation or filtering in dashboards and alerts.
rule.rationale: Each additional label multiplies the number of time series exponentially. Labels that are never used in queries waste memory and slow down scrapes.

rule.statement: Set global scrape interval to 15s. Override to 10s only for critical systems. Use 30–60s for low-priority metrics. Never scrape faster than the metric changes.
rule.rationale: 10s scrape doubles storage and CPU load compared to 15s with minimal benefit for most metrics. Scrape interval below 10s is unsupported in practice — Prometheus needs time to complete each scrape cycle.

rule.statement: Use ServiceMonitor for scraping services by label selector. Use PodMonitor only when pods do not have a backing Service. Define PrometheusRule for all recording rules and alerts.
rule.rationale: ServiceMonitor is the standard Prometheus Operator CRD for service-level scraping. It is self-service — teams can add a ServiceMonitor to their namespace without touching prometheus.yaml. PrometheusRule keeps alerts version-controlled alongside the application.

rule.statement: Pre-compute expensive queries used in dashboards as recording rules. Follow the naming format: level:metric:operation (e.g., job:http_requests:rate5m).
rule.rationale: Dashboard queries running on every panel refresh under load multiply Prometheus CPU usage. Recording rules run once per evaluation interval and store the result as a new time series, making dashboards fast regardless of cardinality.

rule.statement: Alert on symptoms, not causes. Alert on error rate exceeding threshold, not on individual pod failures. Every alert must have a runbook_url annotation.
rule.rationale: Symptom-based alerts (high error rate, high latency) are actionable and describe user impact. Cause-based alerts (pod crashed) create alert fatigue — a pod may crash and restart with no user impact. Runbook URLs eliminate information lookup time during incidents.

rule.statement: Use the for: duration to require alerts to persist before firing. Set at least 2m for critical alerts and 5–15m for warning alerts.
rule.rationale: Transient spikes lasting seconds should not page on-call engineers. A minimum for: duration filters out flapping alerts caused by temporary metric anomalies during deployments or garbage collection.

rule.statement: Use range vectors with rate() for counters. Never use a counter's instant vector value directly in a dashboard — it resets to zero on process restart.
rule.rationale: Counters increase monotonically and reset on restart. rate(counter[5m]) computes per-second rate handling resets automatically. Using the raw counter value shows a sawtooth graph that is meaningless for trend analysis.

rule.statement: Use histogram_quantile() with sum(rate(bucket[5m])) by (le) — always include by (le) and always apply rate() to the bucket series.
rule.rationale: histogram_quantile requires the _bucket series with the le label intact. Without by (le), aggregation collapses buckets incorrectly. Without rate(), a counter-based histogram produces wrong quantiles after resets.
